graph_purpose: Minimal company-to-segment fixture for segment listing, segment counting, and segment membership queries.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
Company-to-segment structure follows Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
No offerings, places, partners, channels, customer types, or revenue models are attached.
Segment names are unique within the company to keep segment lookup and counts unambiguous.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally small and pure so it can support QF01 directly and serve as a clean base for count and membership intent rows in QF20.
The company and segment names are synthetic and chosen to feel realistic without overlapping with any real data source.
If later fixtures need richer structure, they should be added in separate graph instances rather than expanding this one.

graph_purpose: Company-scoped offering fixture for direct offering listing, direct offering counting, and alphabetical ordering at company scope.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
Company-to-offering structure follows Company-[:OFFERS]->Offering at company scope.
No BusinessSegment nodes are included, so these offerings are unambiguously company-scoped.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
Offering names are synthetic and unique within the company to keep list, count, and ordering queries deterministic.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally minimal and fallback-only so it supports QF02 directly without introducing any segment-owned offerings.
The three offerings are named to give a stable alphabetical ordering example for QF21 while still being simple to inspect by eye.
The fixture avoids segments, places, partners, channels, customer types, and revenue models to keep the company-level offering scope pure.

graph_purpose: Canonical segment-owned offering fixture for direct listing, counting, membership, and ordering queries.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The company-to-segment anchor follows Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment.
The segment directly owns three offerings through BusinessSegment-[:OFFERS]->Offering.
No offering hierarchy is present, so direct-offering queries stay one hop from the segment.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
No places, partners, channels, customer types, or revenue models are attached.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally simple and deterministic so QF03 list, count, and membership prompts can be validated by inspection.
Three offerings give the ordering family enough material to test stable sorting without needing any additional graph structure.
The names are synthetic and were chosen to remain distinct while still reading like a plausible product line.

graph_purpose: Segment-anchored offering hierarchy for immediate child lookups, descendant traversal, path anchoring, and parent lookup queries.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The company is anchored to exactly one business segment via Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment.
The offering hierarchy is explicit and tree-shaped, with each child offering having at most one offering parent.
The umbrella offering is segment-anchored, which supports unique path recovery from descendant offerings back to the owning segment.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
No places, partners, channels, customer types, or revenue models are attached, keeping the fixture focused on offering hierarchy traversal.
The sibling offerings give enough breadth for immediate-child lists, descendant traversal, ordering, and count queries.
authoring_notes: This instance is synthetic and intentionally compact so QF04, QF05, QF23, and QF24 can be validated by inspection without extra graph noise.
Northstar Systems and Industrial AI are reused from the other fixture library examples for consistency, while the offering names are synthetic and distinct.
The deeper descendant is attached only through Vision Grid to create a clear parent lookup path without adding a second branch of recursive depth.
No company-level or segment-level direct offerings are added beyond the umbrella root, because the purpose here is hierarchy traversal rather than mixed-scope offering ownership.

graph_purpose: Multi-branch hierarchy fixture for descendant expansion, ancestor recovery, leaf-only traversal, and path anchoring with more than one depth-two branch.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The company is anchored to exactly one business segment via Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment.
The hierarchy is explicit, tree-shaped, and each child offering has at most one offering parent.
The hierarchy root is segment-anchored, so descendant offerings have a unique segment anchor.
Multiple depth-two branches are present, which strengthens ancestor and leaf-descendant supervision without introducing invalid cross-links.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This richer FX04 variant gives us two independent depth-two branches instead of one, which improves descendant counts, leaf-only lists, and ancestor recovery.
Aurelia Nexus acts as a single umbrella offering, while Nexus Insight and Nexus Guard provide distinct parent chains for reverse traversal examples.
Nexus Flow remains a direct leaf child so the graph contains both shallow and deeper descendants in the same tree.

graph_purpose: Company-level geography and partner fixture for place lookup, place counting, partner lookup, partner counting, and existence checks.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
All place nodes use canonical ontology place values and avoid cities or office sites.
The company is linked to places only through Company-[:OPERATES_IN]->Place.
The company is linked to partner companies only through Company-[:PARTNERS_WITH]->Company.
No offerings, segments, customer types, channels, or revenue models are introduced.
Partner nodes are distinct synthetic companies and do not reuse the subject company name.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally minimal so it cleanly supports geography and partnership queries without introducing extra traversal paths.
Three places and three partners give enough variety for list, count, and existence intent examples while staying easy to validate by inspection.
The names are fully synthetic and chosen to remain distinct from the subject company.

graph_purpose: Synthetic segment profile for closed-vocabulary customer-type and channel lookups, plus reverse-lookup and existence-check supervision.
invariants_satisfied: All node labels are ontology-valid and limited to Company, BusinessSegment, CustomerType, Channel, and Offering.
CustomerType and Channel nodes use canonical closed-vocabulary names only.
The company-to-segment structure follows Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment.
The segment connects directly to customer types via SERVES and directly to channels via SELLS_THROUGH.
Offerings are segment-owned only and have no offering-level SELLS_THROUGH edges.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally compact but covers the full FX06 family set through one segment anchor, reverse lookups, and boolean existence checks.
Offerings are included only to make the QF18 join-heavy family concrete; they remain channel-free by design.
The company, segment, offering, and graph identifiers are synthetic and do not correspond to real entities.

graph_purpose: Dual-segment customer-and-channel fixture for reverse lookups, company-filtered segment lookups, and contrastive segment joins.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
CustomerType and Channel nodes use canonical closed-vocabulary names only.
The company anchors two segments via Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment, which supports true reverse lookups and company-filtered segment retrieval.
SERVES and SELLS_THROUGH remain strictly segment-scoped.
Offerings are segment-owned only and no offering-level channel edges are present.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This richer FX06 variant adds a second segment so customer-type and channel reverse lookups can return more than one valid segment.
Manufacturers and system integrators intentionally overlap across the two segments, while government agencies and managed service providers remain segment-specific for contrast.
The direct offerings make QF18-style segment-to-offering joins more realistic without violating the segment-first channel rules.

graph_purpose: Fallback offering fixture for offering-scope channel lookup, channel counting, and channel existence checks without any segment anchor.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The fixture contains no BusinessSegment nodes, so there is no segment anchor.
The company context, if present, is connected only through Company-[:OFFERS]->Offering.
Offering-to-channel structure follows Offering-[:SELLS_THROUGH]->Channel at offering scope.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
Channel names use the canonical ontology vocabulary so fallback channel queries remain valid and deterministic.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally narrow so it can support fallback channel lookup and simple channel existence checks without overlapping with segment-scoped channel fixtures.
The three channels are chosen from the canonical label set to give both list and count examples while keeping the graph easy to inspect.
The company is included only as context and does not introduce any additional scope beyond the allowed OFFERS edge.

graph_purpose: Canonical direct offering-to-revenue-model fixture for monetization listing, counting, and membership queries.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The fixture uses only Offering to RevenueModel monetization edges, as required by the ontology.
Only canonical RevenueModel names from the ontology are used.
No company, segment, place, channel, customer type, or offering hierarchy nodes are introduced.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally minimal so QF11 direct monetization lookup and QF22 existence checks remain unambiguous.
Two revenue models provide enough material for list and count prompts without adding any extra graph branches.
The offering name is synthetic and chosen to feel plausible while staying clearly non-real.

graph_purpose: Richer direct monetization fixture with three revenue models to support list, count, membership, and deduplication behavior.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
Monetization is modeled only with Offering-[:MONETIZES_VIA]->RevenueModel.
Only canonical RevenueModel names from the ontology are used.
No company, segment, place, channel, customer type, or offering hierarchy nodes are introduced.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This variant is richer than the base FX08 instance because it supports three-model list and count behavior without changing the direct-offering query shape.
The combination of subscription, service fees, and licensing is coherent for a synthetic enterprise software offering and gives better contrast for membership queries.
The fixture remains intentionally flat so it does not blur direct monetization with rollup behavior.

graph_purpose: Segment-anchored revenue rollup fixture with an offering hierarchy that supports descendant monetization recovery, filtered rollups, and existence checks.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The company is anchored to exactly one business segment via Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment.
The segment has direct offerings, and the revenue-bearing offering is the family parent rather than a child offering.
MONETIZES_VIA appears only on an Offering node and never on the BusinessSegment or Company.
The offering hierarchy is explicit and tree-shaped, with each child offering having at most one offering parent.
At least one branch in the hierarchy does not monetize, providing contrast for filtered rollups and existence checks.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
No places, partners, channels, or customer types are attached, keeping the fixture focused on revenue rollup behavior.
authoring_notes: This instance is intentionally small but still exercises the key rollup path from Company to BusinessSegment to an offering subtree.
The root offering carries both revenue models, while the child offerings remain non-monetized so descendant traversal must recover monetization from the family parent.
Civic Graph is attached directly to the segment and left without monetization to provide a clear non-monetizing offering branch under the same segment.
Northstar Systems and Industrial AI are reused from the other synthetic fixture examples for consistency, while the offering names remain synthetic and distinct.

graph_purpose: Mixed-root revenue rollup fixture with one hierarchical offering family and one standalone monetized root under the same segment.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The segment is anchored through Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment and owns both a hierarchical offering root and a standalone offering root.
MONETIZES_VIA appears only on offering roots and never on the segment or child offerings.
The hierarchical offering family is explicit and tree-shaped.
At least one descendant branch remains non-monetized while the segment still rolls up multiple revenue models across different roots.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This variant is richer than the base FX09 instance because segment rollup queries must recover revenue models from both a family parent and a separate standalone offering.
Harbor Grid provides the hierarchical rollup path, while Beacon Ledger adds a second direct offering root with its own monetization behavior.
Route Lens and Fleet Prism remain non-monetized children so contributor and filtered-rollup examples can distinguish roots from descendants.

graph_purpose: Synthetic company rollup fixture for company-wide revenue, channel, and customer aggregations across multiple segments, plus count, ordering, and existence supervision.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
Company-to-segment structure follows Company-[:HAS_SEGMENT]->BusinessSegment.
Customer coverage is modeled only with BusinessSegment-[:SERVES]->CustomerType.
Channel coverage is modeled only with BusinessSegment-[:SELLS_THROUGH]->Channel.
Monetization is modeled only with Offering-[:MONETIZES_VIA]->RevenueModel on offering roots, not on child offerings or segments.
Offering hierarchy edges are explicit and each child offering has at most one offering parent.
No company-level convenience rollup triples are materialized.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This instance is designed to support company-wide rollups from multiple segments while staying deterministic enough for count, existence, and ordering queries.
The offering hierarchy is intentionally shallow: one root with children, one root with a child, and one standalone root, so root-level monetization and descendant traversal both remain testable.
The names are fully synthetic and chosen to remain distinct while still matching the sample tone in the fixture library.

graph_purpose: Broader company rollup fixture with overlapping customer, channel, and monetization evidence across four segments.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
Customer coverage remains strictly segment-scoped through SERVES.
Channel coverage remains strictly segment-scoped through SELLS_THROUGH.
Monetization remains strictly offering-scoped and is attached only to offering roots, not to child offerings or segments.
The company contains overlapping customer and channel coverage across multiple segments, which improves company-wide rollup realism.
No company-level convenience triples are materialized.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This richer FX10 variant is intended for actual training coverage, not just seed-authoring, because company rollups now have more overlap, more provenance options, and more than one hierarchy root.

Industrial Platform and Commerce Systems intentionally share large enterprises, while Public Services and Builder Network intentionally share IT professionals to create rollup dedup and provenance contrast.
Orion Stack and Merchant Grid add two different family-root shapes, while Civic Relay and Forge Studio remain standalone monetized offerings.

graph_purpose: Dense synthetic mesh for segment-offering joins, intersections, reverse lookups, filtering, counts, and ordered listings.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
Company-to-segment, segment-to-offering, segment-to-customer, segment-to-channel, and offering-to-revenue structures follow the ontology.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
Some segments satisfy multiple predicates, such as serving a customer type and selling through multiple channels.
Some near-matches do not satisfy the full intersection, such as Commercial Platform not serving developers and Retail Operations not monetizing via advertising.
No offering hierarchy was introduced, so MONETIZES_VIA remains attached directly to offering nodes.
authoring_notes: This graph is intentionally overlap-heavy so it can support joins, filtered counts, intersections, and reverse lookups without needing additional fixture classes.
Industrial AI is the main high-overlap segment for positive `QF16`, `QF17`, `QF18`, and `QF19` style cases.
Retail Operations and Commercial Platform provide near-miss contrast so the model can learn to discriminate matches from non-matches.
The instance stays flat on purpose, because this fixture is meant to test filtering and intersections rather than hierarchy traversal.

graph_purpose: Richer overlap mesh with one shallow hierarchy, multiple shared predicates, and deliberate near-misses for intersection-heavy supervision.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
SERVES and SELLS_THROUGH remain strictly segment-scoped.
MONETIZES_VIA remains strictly offering-scoped and is attached to the hierarchy parent rather than the child offering Deploy Agent.
The graph contains shared predicates and near-misses across segments, which improves intersection and filtering supervision.
The shallow hierarchy is explicit and each child offering has at most one parent offering.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This richer FX11 variant adds one small hierarchy to complement the flat base mesh, which improves robustness on filtered joins without turning the whole fixture into a rollup graph.
Developer Cloud and Enterprise Ops intentionally share IT professionals, while Field Systems and Enterprise Ops intentionally share large enterprises to create multi-hit and near-miss intersections.
Deploy Agent is intentionally non-monetized beneath a monetized family parent so the fixture can exercise hierarchy-aware reasoning without violating the ontology.

graph_purpose: Synthetic ambiguity fixture where the same surface name can plausibly refer to a company, a segment, or an offering, forcing scope-aware refusal or clarification behavior.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
All relationships use ontology-valid edge types only.
The surface name Atlas is reused across multiple valid entities to create ambiguity without invalid structure.
The graph remains queryable because each ambiguous entity still has a valid ontology path and distinct node identity.
The company-to-segment and segment-to-offering structures are both plausible and can support scope-ambiguous requests.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: The primary collision is the repeated surface form Atlas across a company-facing segment and a named offering.
A second company, Atlas Works, adds nearby-name ambiguity for queries that mention Atlas without a scope marker.
The direct company-level OFFERS edge intentionally overlaps with the segment-owned offering path so a naive lookup could choose the wrong scope.
This fixture is intentionally small because QF28 is about disambiguation behavior, not graph size.

graph_purpose: Operational ambiguity fixture where the same surface form can refer to a company, a segment, or a fallback offering with different valid channel answers.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The same surface form Beacon is reused across Company, BusinessSegment, and Offering labels to create scope ambiguity.
The fallback offering Beacon has no business-segment anchor and uses only Offering-[:SELLS_THROUGH]->Channel.
Segment-level channel facts remain strictly on BusinessSegment nodes.
A company-level interpretation of Beacon would validly roll up segment channels only, which differs from both the segment-specific and offering-specific answers.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This fixture is designed for scope-ambiguous channel requests such as How does Beacon sell.

The offering Beacon yields online and marketplaces, the segment Beacon yields direct sales and resellers, and the company Beacon would roll up segment channels including system integrators from Edge Services.
This gives the refusal behavior a real grounding: several different Cypher queries would be valid depending on which Beacon the user meant.

graph_purpose: Operational ambiguity fixture where the same surface form can refer to a company, a segment, or a fallback offering, each with a different valid monetization answer.
invariants_satisfied: All nodes use ontology-valid labels only.
The same surface form Atlas is reused across Company, BusinessSegment, and Offering labels to create real ambiguity.
The fallback offering Atlas is company-scoped only and is not segment-anchored.
The segment Atlas monetizes indirectly through a distinct segment-owned offering, keeping segment rollup behavior ontology-valid.
Company-level monetization would roll up across both Atlas and Harbor Ops segments, which creates a third valid interpretation of the same surface form.
MONETIZES_VIA is attached only to Offering nodes.
No unsupported properties are introduced beyond node_id, label, and name.
authoring_notes: This fixture is designed for ambiguous-name monetization requests such as How does Atlas make money.
The offering Atlas yields subscription, the segment Atlas yields service fees through Atlas Grid, and the company Atlas yields a broader rollup that also includes transaction fees from Harbor Ops.
The point is not to answer the ambiguous question, but to ensure the ambiguity is grounded in genuinely competing valid interpretations.
